_meta.report_name: RBI Sector/Industry-wise Bank Credit — System Model
_meta.status: DRAFT — pending acceptance before replacing system_model.json
review_notes: v2.0 spec: static/dynamic split applied — entity descriptions made structural, current-period numbers removed
signal_ids added to all entity nodes; signal_evidence added to all force nodes
polarity field added to all edges; mechanism replaces label
loops array added: 4 identified loops (2 reinforcing, 2 balancing)
contrasts_with edges retained as structural annotations (polarity: structural)
force_msp_procurement_growth: hypothesis — source is SIBC data, not FCI/CACP external
opp_vehicle_ev: retired — zero sourcing, zero signal_ids
risk_unsecured_migration: reformulated as CIC compliance gap risk
entity_psl aggregate intentionally absent — gap_psl_totals_methodology documents non-additivity

nodes.label: Gold price surge (2023–2026)
nodes.description: A sustained multi-year increase in gold prices in INR terms, driven by global safe-haven demand and rupee depreciation. Increases the effective LTV of gold-backed collateral, expanding the pool of eligible borrowers and the maximum loan amount per gram. Affects all lenders with gold loan exposure. An external commodity price not captured in the SIBC CSV.
nodes.non_observable_reason: An external commodity market price; not a time-series column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source: RBI Press Releases – Sovereign Gold Bond Scheme (March 2023: prid=55327; Feb 2024: prid=57299; Feb 2026 redemption)
nodes.source_excerpt: Gold price per gram: ₹5,611 (Mar 2023) → ₹6,263 (Feb 2024) → ₹15,440 redemption price (Feb 2026)
nodes.source_rationale: RBI SGB redemption prices confirm the price trajectory; LTV expansion mechanism is standard secured lending practice

nodes.label: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
nodes.description: RBI raised risk weights on credit card receivables from 125% to 150% and on consumer credit from 100% to 125% in November 2023. Higher risk weights increase the regulatory capital cost for issuers, compressing origination economics for all unsecured retail products. A policy action with permanent structural effect until reversed.
nodes.non_observable_reason: A discrete regulatory circular event; not a time-series column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source: RBI Circular RBI/2023-24/89, 'Risk Weights for Consumer Credit and Credit Card Receivables', 16 Nov 2023
nodes.source_excerpt: Risk weight on consumer credit increased from 100% to 125%; credit card receivables from 125% to 150%.
nodes.source_rationale: RBI circular directly states the risk-weight changes; compression of credit card and consumer durables YoY growth in subsequent periods confirms the effect

nodes.label: RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024)
nodes.description: RBI revised PSL-eligible housing loan ceilings upward (metro: ₹35L → ₹45L; non-metro: ₹25L → ₹35L). The revision reclassifies existing below-threshold on-book loans into PSL, creating a structural one-time step-up in reported PSL housing credit. Also ripples into export credit eligibility. A policy action that distorts reported growth without representing new lending.
nodes.non_observable_reason: A discrete regulatory amendment to the PSL Master Direction; not a time-series column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source: RBI Master Direction – Priority Sector Lending (Targets and Classification), Amendment Oct 2024
nodes.source_excerpt: Housing loan limits under PSL revised: metro ₹35L→₹45L, non-metro ₹25L→₹35L, effective Oct 2024.
nodes.source_rationale: Amendment directly states the revised ceilings; the reclassification effect is inferred from the step-up in PSL housing data coinciding with the amendment date

nodes.label: PLI scheme capex drawdown phase (FY25–26)
nodes.description: The Production-Linked Incentive scheme created a time-lagged capex investment cycle: approvals in FY22-23 led to active investment and credit drawdowns in FY25-26 due to the typical 2-3 year project implementation lag. Drives simultaneous growth in engineering, specialty chemicals, basic metals, and defence manufacturing credit — sectors that do not normally co-accelerate.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Government scheme implementation phases and approval-to-drawdown lags; not a time-series column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source: PIB Press Note – PLI Scheme: Powering India's Industrial Renaissance, NoteId 155082, March 2025
nodes.source_excerpt: Projects implemented over 2-3 years; by 2025 realized investments rose to ₹1.76 lakh crore as projects shifted to active implementation
nodes.source_rationale: PIB confirms 2-3yr implementation lag and FY25 capex surge; simultaneous strengthening across engineering/chemicals/metals sub-sectors confirms PLI as common driver

nodes.label: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data
nodes.description: Progressive expansion of UDYAM registration and GST transaction data has made MSMEs underwritable at scale for the first time. Banks that invested in MSME origination infrastructure (UDYAM-linked underwriting, GST-based cash flow analysis) can now originate credit at volumes previously unachievable due to information asymmetry. A structural shift in credit market access, not a cyclical phenomenon.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Aggregate improvement in MSME underwritability via registry and tax data; not a time-series column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source: SIDBI MSME Pulse Special Edition Report, June 2025
nodes.source_excerpt: Formal credit penetration rising among UDYAM-registered MSMEs; GST-linked underwriting adopted by major private banks
nodes.source_rationale: SIDBI directly confirms UDYAM + GST as the underwriting mechanism; MSME credit acceleration in SIBC data is the observable effect

nodes.label: NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle
nodes.description: RBI raised risk weights on bank-to-NBFC lending in November 2023, temporarily suppressing bank credit flows to NBFCs. The channel recovery — as banks normalized NBFC exposures within new risk-weight economics — amplifies all NBFC-intermediated lending across sectors. January SIBC files do not report NBFC sub-classification; verification limited to March-end periods.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Aggregate NBFC channel capacity and credit availability; not measurable as a single isolated column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source_excerpt: RBI/2023-24/85 – Regulatory measures towards consumer credit and bank-NBFC exposures (Nov 2023)
nodes.source_rationale: Risk weight circular verified; channel recovery claim needs NBFC-specific data to promote to inference. Cycle 1 of hypothesis clock starts at this FOUNDATION.

nodes.label: MSP procurement volumes growing year-on-year
nodes.description: Government MSP procurement agencies (FCI, state agencies) draw increasing bank credit each kharif season to finance grain purchases. As MSP prices and procurement quantities grow each year, the food credit cycle's peak and trough both rise — creating a secular upward trend on top of the mechanistic seasonal pattern. NEEDS SOURCING: current evidence is the SIBC data itself (the effect). Requires FCI Annual Report or CACP MSP press release as external force evidence.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Government procurement volumes and MSP price decisions are external policy inputs; not a time-series column in the SIBC CSV
nodes.source: RBI SIBC series — food credit sub-category (note: this is the effect, not external force evidence)
nodes.source_excerpt: Food credit shows mechanistic seasonal pattern with secular growth across periods
nodes.source_rationale: SIBC data confirms the seasonal pattern and secular trend; FCI/CACP external source needed to promote to inference. Cycle 1 of hypothesis clock starts at this FOUNDATION.

nodes.label: Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24)
nodes.description: Large infrastructure projects (highways, ports, power, urban) awarded in FY22-24 are transitioning from the construction/drawdown phase to the operations/repayment phase. This structural shift suppresses new credit origination even as existing project books remain large, creating a net drag on infrastructure credit growth. The next capex cycle (renewables, data centres) has not yet scaled to replace it.
nodes.non_observable_reason: Infrastructure project lifecycle phases are not captured as a CSV column; the transition is visible only through aggregate credit deceleration
nodes.source: RBI SIBC merged series (Jan 2024 – Apr 2026) — Infrastructure sub-sector YoY
nodes.source_excerpt: Infrastructure credit FY growth sustained below system average across ≥3 consecutive periods
nodes.source_rationale: Multi-period underperformance pattern is consistent with the operations-phase rotation hypothesis; CRISIL/ICRA infrastructure sector report would strengthen external evidence

nodes.label: Total Bank Credit
nodes.description: The aggregate of all credit extended by scheduled commercial banks. Acts as the system-level output signal for the credit cycle. Composed of food credit and non-food credit, further decomposed into agriculture, industry, services, and personal loans. Responds positively to economic growth, bank liquidity, and regulatory conditions.

nodes.label: Food Credit
nodes.description: Bank credit extended to government food procurement agencies (FCI, state agencies) for MSP grain purchases. Follows a mechanistic seasonal cycle: peaks at March-end (kharif procurement drawdown), collapses to near-zero or negative at January (post-repayment). Secular growth tracks annual expansion in MSP procurement volumes and prices.

nodes.label: Industry credit (sector aggregate)
nodes.description: Bank credit to the manufacturing and industrial sector. Responds positively to capex cycles, PLI scheme drawdowns, and MSME formalisation. Sensitive to infrastructure project pipeline depth and large corporate investment appetite. Decomposes into MSME (micro/small/medium) and large corporate sub-segments, further divided by industry type sub-sectors.

nodes.label: Services credit (sector aggregate)
nodes.description: Bank credit to the services sector. Includes NBFCs, computer software, trade, commercial real estate, transport, tourism, and other service providers. Partially subject to NBFC double-counting (see gap_nbfc_double_counting). Decomposes into sub-sectors with heterogeneous growth dynamics.

nodes.label: Personal Loans (sector aggregate)
nodes.description: Bank credit to individuals and households. The largest sector by outstanding. Decomposes into housing, gold loans, vehicle loans, credit cards, education, consumer durables, and other sub-categories. Aggregate growth masks significant internal divergence — the sub-category composition is the analytically meaningful signal.

nodes.label: Micro & Small Enterprises
nodes.description: Bank credit to micro and small enterprises. Responds positively to UDYAM registration coverage, GST data availability, and bank investment in MSME origination infrastructure. Subject to MSME size definition ambiguity across SIBC sub-categories (see gap_size_definition). A leading indicator of MSME economic formalisation progress.

nodes.label: Large Corporate credit
nodes.description: Bank credit to large corporate borrowers. Responds to capex cycles, infrastructure project pipelines, and balance sheet capacity of large industrial groups. Grows more slowly than MSME in periods of MSME formalisation-driven credit expansion. A measure of the high-end industrial investment cycle.

nodes.label: Medium Enterprises
nodes.description: Bank credit to medium enterprises. Positioned between the high-growth MSME segment and the more mature large corporate segment. Benefits from both MSME formalisation tailwinds (when medium enterprises upgrade from small) and corporate-grade credit facilities.

nodes.label: All Engineering (industry sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to the all-engineering industry sub-sector, covering manufacturing of electrical/electronic equipment, industrial machinery, defence components, and EV parts. Sensitive to PLI scheme capex drawdown cycles and upstream supply chain investment from metals and chemicals sectors.

nodes.label: Infrastructure credit (industry sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to infrastructure projects including roads, ports, power, and urban infrastructure. Responds positively to government capex pipeline and project drawdown phases; negatively to project completion and repayment phases. Historically a large share of industry credit; currently subject to the operations-phase transition.

nodes.label: Chemicals & Petroleum Products (industry sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to chemicals, petrochemicals, and petroleum products manufacturers. Responds to PLI specialty chemicals schemes and upstream demand from engineering sector borrowers. An input sector to engineering manufacturing.

nodes.label: Basic Metals & Metal Products (industry sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to iron, steel, aluminium, and metal products manufacturers. Responds to PLI defence and EV component schemes and engineering sector upstream supply chain demand.

nodes.label: Computer Software & IT Services (services sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to IT services and computer software companies. Responds to global IT sector growth and acquisition financing. Infrastructure-agnostic — does not depend on physical capital investment cycles that affect manufacturing or infrastructure sub-sectors.

nodes.label: Trade (Wholesale & Retail, services sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to wholesale and retail trade businesses. A structural volume driver within services credit — trade finance (inventory, receivables) is a recurring working capital need. Decomposes into wholesale and retail sub-categories with different sensitivity to economic conditions.

nodes.label: Transport Operators (services sub-sector)
nodes.description: Bank credit to transport and logistics operators. Sensitive to fleet expansion cycles and logistics sector capacity utilisation. Subject to post-COVID normalisation patterns as fleet additions made in 2021-22 are absorbed.

nodes.label: Gold Loans (retail sub-category)
nodes.description: Secured retail lending against physical gold collateral. Demand responds positively to collateral values and negatively to the availability of unsecured credit alternatives. Supply is operationally constrained by bank infrastructure (branch-level assay, safe custody, cash management). A key product in the secured retail credit segment.

nodes.label: Credit Card Outstanding (retail sub-category)
nodes.description: Revolving unsecured retail credit. Demand driven by consumer spending needs and credit availability. Supply economics are highly sensitive to regulatory risk-weight treatment — a 25pp risk-weight increase has a direct and quantifiable impact on issuer return on capital. A leading indicator of retail credit cycle health and regulatory posture.

nodes.label: Vehicle Loans (retail sub-category)
nodes.description: Secured retail lending for vehicle purchases. Responds positively to auto sector sales growth and vehicle registrations. Covers both ICE and emerging EV vehicle financing. A consumer durables sub-category with secured collateral characteristics.

nodes.label: Consumer Durables credit (retail sub-category)
nodes.description: Short-tenure credit for consumer electronics and appliance purchases. Highly elastic to issuer profitability conditions — the risk-weight treatment determines whether consumer finance lending is economically viable for banks. The most direct transmission channel for RBI risk-weight policy into retail credit.

nodes.label: PSL Housing (priority sector sub-category)
nodes.description: Bank credit to housing borrowers classified under Priority Sector Lending. Growth responds directly to RBI revisions of PSL housing loan eligibility ceilings — upward ceiling revisions reclassify existing sub-threshold loans into PSL, creating reported growth that does not represent new lending (see gap_psl_reclassification).

nodes.label: Export Credit (priority sector sub-category)
nodes.description: Bank credit to export businesses classified under PSL. Sensitive to PSL eligibility threshold revisions and global trade conditions. The contraction in export credit following the Oct 2024 PSL revision reflects a ripple effect of ceiling changes on eligibility classification.

nodes.label: January SIBC missing Gold, Housing, NBFC, Tourism sub-classifications
nodes.description: January SIBC files structurally omit sub-classifications for Gold Loans, PSL Housing, NBFC credit, and Tourism. These series appear only in March files. January-to-January YoY comparisons for these categories are not computable, limiting the range of signals available in the majority of reporting periods.

nodes.label: PSL housing growth conflates reclassification with new lending
nodes.description: Following ceiling revisions, SIBC data cannot separate reclassified existing loans from genuinely new PSL-eligible origination. The reported growth number is analytically misleading — it includes a one-time stock reclassification that inflates the flow measure.
nodes.source: RBI PSL Amendment Oct 2024 + SIBC data step-up pattern
nodes.source_excerpt: Housing loan limits revised: metro ₹35L→₹45L, non-metro ₹25L→₹35L, effective Oct 2024
nodes.source_rationale: Single-period step-up magnitude with no new demand catalyst confirms reclassification as the primary driver

nodes.label: NBFC flows double-counted across services and end-use sectors
nodes.description: NBFC credit is reported as a services sub-category in SIBC, but NBFC on-lending reaches all sectors (retail, MSME, housing, vehicle). When NBFC credit accelerates, it inflates reported services growth while the actual economic activity is distributed elsewhere. Services credit is structurally overstated during NBFC expansion phases.

nodes.label: PSL sub-category totals are non-additive
nodes.description: PSL sub-categories (agriculture, MSME, housing, renewable, etc.) are not additive to a PSL total because individual loans can qualify under multiple categories and different targets apply to different bank types. A parent entity_psl aggregate node is intentionally absent from this model — adding it would imply an additive total exists, contradicting this gap.
nodes.source: RBI Master Direction — Priority Sector Lending
nodes.source_excerpt: PSL targets are bank-type specific; sub-category overlaps exist in classification
nodes.source_rationale: RBI Master Direction confirms different target structures per bank type; overlap creates non-additivity

nodes.label: MSME size definitions differ across SIBC sub-categories
nodes.description: SIBC applies the revised June 2020 MSME definition (turnover-based) for PSL but the earlier investment-based definition for industry credit sub-categories. Micro and Small under PSL and under Industry credit are not the same population. Cross-series comparison overstates apparent formalisation coverage.
nodes.source: MSME Ministry Notification June 1 2020 (Atmanirbhar revised definition)
nodes.source_excerpt: Revised MSME classification based on turnover and investment criteria effective June 2020
nodes.source_rationale: Different effective dates and criteria confirmed by regulatory text

nodes.label: Gold loan market entry / deepening for banks
nodes.description: Banks are taking share from NBFCs in gold loans, with banks growing faster. The product moat is operational (branch-level assay, safe custody, cash management), not regulatory — banks with branch networks can sustain competitive advantage. The growth trajectory is structural (collateral-driven), not speculative.
nodes.source: ICRA Research Report – Non-banking Financial Companies Gold Loans, September 2024
nodes.source_excerpt: Banks expanded at 26% CAGR vs NBFCs at 18% (FY2020-FY2024); PSBs share rose from 54% (Mar 2019) to 63% (Mar 2024)
nodes.source_rationale: ICRA data confirms bank share gain; operational moat inference supported by entry barrier structure

nodes.label: MSME origination infrastructure investment window
nodes.description: Banks that invested in MSME origination infrastructure (UDYAM-linked underwriting, GST-based cash flow analysis, co-lending rails) over 2022-24 are in a harvesting phase now. The competitive window for catch-up investment is approximately 2-3 years before the first-cycle cohort matures and saturation limits further marginal returns. Banks without this infrastructure will structurally underperform in MSME credit growth.
nodes.source: SIDBI MSME Pulse Special Edition Report, June 2025
nodes.source_excerpt: Formal credit penetration rising among UDYAM-registered MSMEs; GST-linked underwriting adopted by major private banks
nodes.source_rationale: SIDBI report confirms penetration trend; 2-3yr window is an inference from typical credit cycle maturation timing

nodes.label: PLI anchor-supplier chain finance
nodes.description: PLI scheme recipients have validated anchor relationships with tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers. Supply chain finance structures (invoice discounting, vendor finance, dealer finance) can be layered on these anchor-supplier relationships at lower credit risk than standalone unsecured MSME lending, because the cash flow is backed by a large anchor company.
nodes.source: PIB Press Release – PLI Scheme, PRID: 155082, August 2025
nodes.source_excerpt: By 2025 realized investments rose to ₹1.76 lakh crore as projects shifted to active implementation
nodes.source_rationale: PLI investment phase creates anchor-supplier relationships; SCF structuring is a standard inference from this supply chain visibility

nodes.label: EV-specific vehicle credit products
nodes.description: RETIRED at first FOUNDATION (2026-06-10). No external source verified for EV share of vehicle loans or EV-specific credit risk differentiation. No L1 signal separating EV from ICE vehicle loans. Returns only when EV share of vehicle loans is measurable in L1 (e.g., VAHAN registration data ingestion).
nodes.source_rationale: Zero sourcing, zero signal_ids, zero annotation_ids. Cannot persist across FOUNDATION cycles.

nodes.label: Renewable energy project finance — next capex wave
nodes.description: As the infrastructure supercycle completes, the next infrastructure credit wave is in solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects. Banks with project finance capability built on traditional infrastructure can redeploy that expertise. Government renewable energy targets create a regulatory pipeline that de-risks the opportunity.
nodes.source: PIB Press Release PRID 1888547 – Cabinet approves National Green Hydrogen Mission, January 4, 2023
nodes.source_excerpt: National Green Hydrogen Mission approved with target of 5 MMT annual production by 2030
nodes.source_rationale: Government renewable targets create a regulatory pipeline; bank redirection from traditional infra to renewables is an inference from capacity redeployment logic

nodes.label: PSLC market depth from PSL reclassification surplus
nodes.description: Banks that received a large reclassification-driven PSL surplus from the Oct 2024 ceiling revision can sell PSL Certificates to banks that are under-achieving on PSL targets. This deepens the PSLC secondary market and creates a one-time monetisation event for well-positioned banks.
nodes.source: RBI Master Direction – Priority Sector Lending; RBI PSLC guidelines (2016)
nodes.source_excerpt: Banks may sell PSL Certificates representing achievement of PSL targets to other scheduled commercial banks
nodes.source_rationale: PSLC mechanism confirmed; reclassification surplus creating a supply event is inferred from the Oct 2024 revision magnitude

nodes.label: CIC reporting compliance gaps creating off-balance-sheet credit risk
nodes.description: RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2022/2025) require BNPL and digital credit to report to Credit Information Companies. However, compliance may be incomplete among newer digital lenders operating under first-loss guarantee structures or co-lending arrangements where the regulated entity's on-book exposure is partially shielded. If reporting gaps exist, the true unsecured credit stress is understated in bank balance sheets and in SIBC data.
nodes.source_rationale: Reformulated at first FOUNDATION. Original 'untracked channels' framing contradicted RBI DLG which mandates CIC reporting. Reformulated as CIC compliance gap in newer digital lenders — plausible but needs RBI Annual Report 2024-25 fintech compliance section as source. Cycle 1 of hypothesis clock.

nodes.label: Infrastructure repayment wave with insufficient new origination pipeline
nodes.description: Banks over-indexed to traditional infrastructure credit face a structural squeeze: the FY22-24 project book moves to repayment (compressing balances) while the next-cycle pipeline (renewables, data centres) is slower to ramp. Banks without next-cycle project finance capability face credit portfolio contraction.
nodes.source: RBI SIBC merged series — Infrastructure sub-sector YoY and FY acceleration signals
nodes.source_excerpt: Infrastructure credit FY growth sustained below system average across ≥3 consecutive periods
nodes.source_rationale: Multi-period deceleration pattern is consistent with operations-phase rotation

edges.mechanism: Higher collateral values expand LTV headroom, enlarging the eligible borrower pool and maximum loan amount per gram
Structural collateral-driven growth provides a durable market rationale for bank entry and deepening over NBFC positions
Risk weight hike from 125% to 150% raises regulatory capital cost per unit of credit card exposure, directly compressing origination economics
Risk weight hike from 100% to 125% eliminates consumer finance lending economics for most bank issuers
Suppressed formal unsecured credit creates an incentive for newer digital lenders to absorb displaced demand with incomplete regulatory compliance
Households unable to access or expand unsecured revolving credit shift toward secured gold collateral borrowing for similar liquidity needs
Sustained contraction in consumer durables credit pushes demand toward less-regulated digital lending channels
Persistent below-10% credit card growth signals demand accumulation in channels with potentially incomplete CIC reporting
Ceiling revision reclassifies existing sub-threshold loans into PSL, creating a step-up in reported PSL housing credit that is not new lending
Reclassification makes true new PSL lending indistinguishable from existing stock reclassified; the growth number cannot be decomposed without bank-level data
Reclassification event exposes and amplifies the non-additive nature of PSL sub-category reporting
PSL revision ripples into export credit eligibility threshold calculations, altering what qualifies
Reclassification surplus creates a PSLC supply event for banks that are over-achieving on PSL post-revision
PSL housing growth metric is analytically misleading without separating reclassification from new origination
PLI capex drawdown phase drives engineering credit as recipient firms invest in electronics, defence, and EV component manufacturing
PLI specialty chemicals schemes drive upstream capex credit as firms invest in speciality chemical capacity
PLI defence and EV schemes create upstream demand for metals as supply chain investment accelerates
Validated anchor-supplier relationships created by PLI investment enable SCF structures at lower risk than standalone MSME lending
Upstream specialty chemicals supply chain enables engineering manufacturing production capacity; chemicals credit growth predicts engineering credit expansion
Steel, aluminium, and metals supply chain feeds engineering and defence manufacturing — upstream metals credit is a leading input to downstream engineering capex
UDYAM registration and GST transaction history make MSMEs underwritable at scale; banks with origination infrastructure convert this registry access into credit origination
Progressive adoption of different MSME definitions across SIBC and PSL series creates a cross-series comparison gap that compounds over time
Banks with UDYAM-linked underwriting infrastructure built over 2022-24 are in a harvesting window — origination at scale is now possible where it wasn't before
Structural observation: MSME and large corporate are on opposite growth trajectories in the current period, reflecting different demand drivers within the same industry credit envelope
Structural observation: Micro and small growing materially faster than medium, suggesting formalisation benefits accrue first to smaller enterprises
Sustained MSME credit growth confirms the commercial case for origination infrastructure investment; the opportunity window is quantifiable from the growth trajectory
NBFC channel reopening amplifies all NBFC-intermediated lending, which is reported under services in SIBC regardless of the actual end-use sector
NBFC channel expansion amplifies the double-counting gap — NBFC credit counted in services but economic activity distributed across sectors
Larger MSP procurement volumes and higher MSP prices each kharif season require larger bank credit drawdowns by FCI and state procurement agencies
Operations phase replaces construction-phase credit drawdowns with principal repayments; net credit balance declines unless new project origination offsets repayments
Supercycle completion creates a structural squeeze for banks over-indexed to infrastructure without a next-cycle origination pipeline
Government renewable targets create the next infrastructure credit pipeline; banks with project finance capability can redeploy from traditional infra
Sustained deceleration with high existing exposure creates balance sheet management pressure for infrastructure-heavy lenders
Gold loans are not reported in January SIBC files — removes this sub-category from the majority of reporting periods' YoY comparison
Vehicle loan growth includes an emerging EV component — retired until EV share is measurable in L1 data
Engineering sub-sector surge is the primary driver of industry credit re-acceleration; engineering growth rate above system average lifts the sector aggregate
Trade finance is a recurring structural driver of services credit — working capital cycles in trade generate consistent credit demand within the services aggregate

loops.label: PLI Supply Chain Reinforcing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes externally as production volume growth leads to approval of subsequent PLI cohorts, sustaining the investment cycle
loops.description: PLI capex investment drives metals and chemicals credit, which feed engineering manufacturing credit growth, which drives overall industry credit. As production capacity grows and PLI targets are achieved, subsequent policy cohorts sustain and extend the cycle.

loops.label: MSME Formalisation Reinforcing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes externally as successful MSME credit access enables business expansion and upstream supplier formalisation, compounding the registry effect
loops.description: MSME formalisation via UDYAM + GST data makes MSMEs underwritable, driving credit growth. Successful credit access enables MSME business expansion and formalization of additional supply chain participants. The growing pool of UDYAM-registered MSMEs reinforces the origination opportunity.

loops.label: Unsecured Credit Rerouting Balancing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes externally when regulatory pressure eases (risk weights reversed) or when secured credit capacity saturates, allowing unsecured credit to recover
loops.description: Risk-weight pressure suppresses unsecured credit (credit cards, consumer durables). Displaced household demand reroutes to secured gold loans. As secured credit absorbs the displaced demand, the overall retail credit mix rebalances. The pressure creates its own relief valve — a balancing loop that dampens the impact on total retail credit.

loops.label: Infrastructure Cycle Transition Balancing Loop
loops.closure_note: Closes as renewable energy and next-generation infrastructure investment creates a new capex cycle that absorbs the capacity displaced by traditional infra repayment
loops.description: Infrastructure supercycle completion suppresses traditional infra credit, creating repayment pressure for banks. This same capital redeployment creates a renewable energy project finance opportunity — the next infrastructure wave. The balancing character: as traditional infra contracts, the conditions for the next capex cycle strengthen.
